content.rendered:
IT took its literal inspiration from an infamously dangerous Australian highway and its figurative inspiration from the touring life of a rock band.

But when lead vocalist and co-songwriter Bon Scott died from an overdose just six months after the song’s release, “Highway to Hell” took on far more sinister meaning.

A favorite of heavy metal fans ever since its original release in 1979, “Highway to Hell” became a signature song for AC/DC, with an immediately recognizable riff that’s a contender for rock’s greatest.

It peaked only at #47 on the Billboard Hot 100, but it became an international hit, charting in the band’s native Australia and all across Europe. It eventually went multi-platinum around the world, including a 6x certification in the U.S.

Rolling Stone ranks it #258 on their 500 Greatest Songs of All Time list, and the Rock and Roll Hall of Fame enshrined it as one of their 500 Songs that Shaped Rock and Roll.

Even today, it appears in far too many movies and television shows to mention, winning the “Most Played Australian Work Overseas” award at the 2009 APRA Awards.

So when Bruce Springsteen took the E Street Band down under in 2014, he knew just what song to surprise his Australian fans with

Taking the stage in darkness in Perth (Bon Scott’s hometown) on the third night of a tour that had already proved unpredictable from night to night, even by Springsteen’s standards, Bruce played that iconic riff. The arena went instantly wild.

The crowd was electrified, and so was the band–especially Tom Morello (on loan to the E Street Band for this tour while Little Steven was off filming a television series), whose joy was palpable.

It was too good a performance to stay a one-off, so Bruce brought it back the following show to open the encore set. The band was even tighter this time, with Bruce, Nils, and Tom all turning in inspired guitar solos.

So how to you top a surprise opener and a surprise encore with a third performance? You invite a surprise guest. When the tour moved on to Melbourne a few days later, Bruce invited Eddie Vedder to share lead vocals on “Highway to Hell.”

“Highway to Hell” made a few more appearances on the tour after that (including a pair of outings stateside), but after that it was seemingly retired from Bruce’s repertoire.

But then seven years later, along came a brand new studio recording of “Highway to Hell,” issued by Tom Morello with lead vocals by both Springsteen and Vedder.

Recorded remotely during the pandemic, Morello’s studio version can’t quite capture the lightning in a bottle from those 2014 arena performances, but it’s still a great recording and an undeservedly under-the-radar official Springsteen recording.

Highway to Hell
Recorded: 2021
Released: The Atlas Underground Fire (2021)
First performed: February 8, 2014 (Perth, Australia)
Last performed: April 29, 2014 (Sunrise, FL)

© July 15 ,2026

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: In Michigan
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: In a bolt of pre-show inspiration, Bruce wrote one of his all-time greatest songs for an appreciative Michigan audience.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Cover Me, Air Supply: 4th of July, Asbury Park (Sandy)
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: Did a surprising E Street through-line inspire Air Supply's 1983 cover of Bruce's 1973 classic?

jetpack-related-posts.title: Roll of the Dice: Losin’ Kind
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: "Losin' Kind" is one of only two original Nebraska demos left in the vault, but its imprint can be found across Bruce's catalog.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Highway 29
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: “Highway 29” is a taut, cinematic masterpiece with some of Bruce’s best lyrics that challenge the listener to co-write the song.

jetpack-related-posts.title: RotD/MatR: Joe Grushecky and Bruce Springsteen: Homestead
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: One of Joe and Bruce's best collaborations may have inspired one of Bruce's own songs too.

jetpack-related-posts.title: Key to the Highway
jetpack-related-posts.author: Ken
jetpack-related-posts.excerpt: The Bruce Springsteen Band delivers a swinging take on a blues classic.
